stem: Consider \( f(x) = x^{2} + [[a]]x + [[b]] \) as a polynomial over the finite field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) (the field of integers modulo the prime [[p]]). For how many elements \( k \in \{0, 1, \dots, [[p]]-1\} \) is \( f(k) \equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \)?
solution: A degree-2 polynomial over a FIELD has at most 2 roots, so we test every residue \( k = 0, \dots, [[p]]-1 \) and count how many satisfy \( k^{2} + [[a]]k + [[b]] \equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \). Direct substitution gives exactly \( [[answer]] \) such \( k \). (If this count is \( 0 \), \( f \) has no root in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), so — since it is degree 2 — it is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \).)
distractor_rationales: This assumes every nonzero residue but one works, wildly overcounting roots for a degree-2 polynomial.
This is the size of the whole field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \), not the number of roots of \( f \) inside it.
This exceeds the maximum possible number of roots for a degree-2 polynomial over a field (which is 2).
This assumes exactly one root exists without checking every residue.
This assumes the polynomial always splits into two distinct roots, which is not checked here.
This assumes \( f \) has no roots without checking every residue mod [[p]].
This overcounts the true root count by one.

stem: Consider \( g(x) = x^{3} + [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \) as a polynomial over the finite field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). For how many elements \( k \in \{0, 1, \dots, [[p]]-1\} \) is \( g(k) \equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \)?
solution: A degree-3 polynomial over a field has at most 3 roots. Checking every residue \( k = 0,\dots,[[p]]-1 \) for \( g(k) \equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \) gives exactly \( [[answer]] \) roots. (A cubic is reducible over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) exactly when it has at least one root there, since any factorization of a cubic must include a linear factor; so a count of \( 0 \) here means \( g \) is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \).)
distractor_rationales: This overcounts by assuming almost every residue is a root of a degree-3 polynomial.
This is the size of the field itself, not the number of roots \( g \) has in it.
This exceeds the maximum possible number of roots of a cubic over a field (which is 3).
This assumes exactly one root without checking every residue mod [[p]].
This assumes exactly two roots without checking every residue mod [[p]].
This assumes the cubic has no roots at all without checking every residue.
This overcounts the true root count by one.

stem: Let \( h(x) = x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \), a polynomial with integer coefficients. Using the discriminant \( \Delta = [[b]]^{2} - 4([[c]]) = [[disc]] \), determine whether \( h(x) \) is IRREDUCIBLE over the field of rational numbers \( \mathbb{Q} \). Enter \( 1 \) if it is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Q} \), or \( 0 \) if it factors into linear factors over \( \mathbb{Q} \).
solution: A monic quadratic over \( \mathbb{Q} \) is irreducible exactly when it has no rational root, i.e. exactly when its discriminant \( \Delta = b^{2}-4c \) is EITHER negative (no real root) OR positive but not a perfect square (irrational roots). Here \( \Delta = [[disc]] \), so \( h(x) \) is irreducible over \( \mathbb{Q} \) exactly when the answer is \( 1 \); computing directly gives \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the flipped verdict — it swaps the irreducible/reducible cases.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.
This is not a valid 0/1 verdict at all — the answer must be exactly 0 or 1.

stem: Consider \( f(x) = x^{4} + [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]] \) as a polynomial over the finite field \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \). For how many elements \( k \in \{0, 1, \dots, [[p]]-1\} \) is \( f(k) \equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \)?
solution: A degree-4 polynomial over a field has at most 4 roots. Checking every residue \( k=0,\dots,[[p]]-1 \) for \( f(k)\equiv 0 \pmod{[[p]]} \) gives exactly \( [[answer]] \) roots. (Note: unlike degree \( \le 3 \), a quartic with NO roots in \( \mathbb{Z}_{[[p]]} \) is not automatically irreducible there — it could still split into two irreducible quadratic factors — but the root count itself is exactly what direct substitution gives.)
distractor_rationales: This overcounts by assuming almost every residue is a root of a degree-4 polynomial.
This is the size of the field itself, not the number of roots \( f \) has in it.
This exceeds the maximum possible number of roots of a quartic over a field (which is 4).
This assumes exactly one root without checking every residue mod [[p]].
This assumes exactly two roots without checking every residue mod [[p]].
This assumes the quartic has no roots at all without checking every residue.
This overcounts the true root count by one.
